coredata.dc:description: This work aims to apply carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from agriculture cellulosic waste (agro wastes), produced via an economically and eco-friendly single-step method, to be used into cellulose acetate composite microfibrous membranes as an innovative solution specifically designed to adsorb methylene blue (MB) and other cationic dyes that are present in various water effluents. Batch adsorption tests were conducted, with variations in contact time (1–24 h), initial MB concentration (25–300 ppm), and adsorbent doses (1–20 g/L). The maximum adsorption capacity of the membrane was 198 mg/g with an initial concentration of 300 ppm at 298 K. Thermodynamic parameters showed that the process is endothermic. Equilibrium experimental data for MB adsorption onto electrospun adsorbent were fitted using different isothermal models, with the Freundlich model showing the best fit. The pseudo-second-order model accurately described the kinetic data with high reliability (R2 > 0.99), and the calculated adsorption capacity was very close to the experimental data. N-CQDs loaded membranes were also tested for removing methyl violet and rhodamine B, demonstrating remarkably high dye removal efficiency. The underlying adsorption mechanism was also reported. Finally, it is worth mentioning that composite adsorbents can be efficiently applied to actual industrial cases because of the possibility of reusing them, opening the route to the fabrication of novel and highly performant adsorbents. These findings underscore N-CQDs’ effectiveness in enhancing pollutant removal efficiency from wastewater, highlighting their environmental benefits and promoting a more sustainable approach to water treatment. Therefore, the prepared adsorbent, showing excellent adsorption performance, places them among adsorbents for practical applications in wastewater purification.

head.ce:title: Application of electrospun N-doped carbon dots loaded cellulose acetate membranes as cationic dyes adsorbent

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work aims to apply carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from agriculture cellulosic waste (agro wastes), produced via an economically and eco-friendly single-step method, to be used into cellulose acetate composite microfibrous membranes as an innovative solution specifically designed to adsorb methylene blue (MB) and other cationic dyes that are present in various water effluents. Batch adsorption tests were conducted, with variations in contact time (1–24 h), initial MB concentration (25–300 ppm), and adsorbent doses (1–20 g/L). The maximum adsorption capacity of the membrane was 198 mg/g with an initial concentration of 300 ppm at 298 K. Thermodynamic parameters showed that the process is endothermic. Equilibrium experimental data for MB adsorption onto electrospun adsorbent were fitted using different isothermal models, with the Freundlich model showing the best fit. The pseudo-second-order model accurately described the kinetic data with high reliability (R2 > 0.99), and the calculated adsorption capacity was very close to the experimental data. N-CQDs loaded membranes were also tested for removing methyl violet and rhodamine B, demonstrating remarkably high dye removal efficiency. The underlying adsorption mechanism was also reported. Finally, it is worth mentioning that composite adsorbents can be efficiently applied to actual industrial cases because of the possibility of reusing them, opening the route to the fabrication of novel and highly performant adsorbents. These findings underscore N-CQDs’ effectiveness in enhancing pollutant removal efficiency from wastewater, highlighting their environmental benefits and promoting a more sustainable approach to water treatment. Therefore, the prepared adsorbent, showing excellent adsorption performance, places them among adsorbents for practical applications in wastewater purification.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite the Earth's abundance of water, only a fraction is suitable for consumption. The pressing issue of water scarcity, exacerbated by the growing population, necessitates immediate action (F et al., 2022). The key to addressing these concerns is treating polluted water, removing contaminants, and converting them into harmless substances. Various physical, chemical, and biological techniques and integrated systems are widely utilized in wastewater treatment for safe disposal or reuse (Abbaz et al., 2023; Ahmed et al., 2021; Viscusi et al., 2021). Wastewater treatment is pivotal as it strives to reduce most suspended solids significantly. This objective is accomplished through a two-tiered treatment approach: primary treatments, which eliminate the larger suspended solids, and secondary treatments, which facilitate the removal of smaller suspended solids and dissolved organic matter (Massoud et al., 2009). The primary chemical treatment process utilizes coagulant and flocculant chemicals to efficiently eliminate suspended solids, organic carbon, and nutrients from wastewater. Commonly used coagulants in primary chemical treatment include alum and iron salts (Maćczak et al., 2020; Shewa and Dagnew, 2020). Various anionic, cationic, and neutral polymers are employed for flocculants, with polyaluminium chloride (Maćczak et al., 2020) and polyacrylamide (Rabiee et al., 2014) being the most used. However, in many cases, primary treatment alone is inadequate to protect receiving water or provide water with properties suitable for industrial and domestic use. As a result, additional steps have been incorporated into the wastewater treatment process to enhance the removal of organic matter, solids, nutrients, and toxic substances (Saravanan et al., 2021). Advanced wastewater treatments have therefore been proposed as a global priority to preserve essential resources for future use. Among the various advanced wastewater treatments, those based on the use of membranes are particularly promising due to their unique characteristics in terms of selective permeability of the membranes. It is, therefore, possible to separate the pollutants from the aqueous matrix based on size, charge, and other physicochemical properties (Ezugbe and Rathilal, 2020). Membranes act as selective barriers, allowing molecules to permeate while hindering the passage of unwanted contaminants. Water purification necessitates systems with precisely engineered porosity and customized morphologies, considering that the pollutants contained in wastewater have different sizes and functionalities (Abdulhamid and Muzamil, 2023). Previous studies in the literature have widely discussed the potential of membrane technology in wastewater treatment, highlighting its separation efficiency, selectivity, easily achievable operating conditions, the absence of chemical modifications, and versatility in managing the different characteristics of pollutants (Al Aani et al., 2020; Hube et al., 2020; Bera et al., 2022). Membrane processes also have better stability than conventional processes, as their efficiency does not depend particularly on water quality. The main membrane processes used in wastewater treatments are microfiltration, ultrafiltration, nanofiltration, electrodialysis, and reverse osmosis (Ahmed et al., 2022). Polymeric membranes, one of the many available types, can be prepared using materials that can be processed in solution. They are produced in various forms, such as fiber mats, hollow fibers, and flat-sheet membranes (Adam et al., 2022; Hosseini et al., 2016). Electrospinning is a method for efficiently synthesizing nanofibrous membranes with a submicrometric structure and interconnected pores (Viscusi, 2023; Bugatti et al., 2018; Viscusi et al., 2023). This method uses high voltage to create an electric field between a polymeric solution droplet coming from a nozzle and a collector plate. Process parameters, solution characteristics, and ambient conditions are crucial in determining membrane characteristics and performances. A smart approach to expanding the polymeric fibers' functionalities for water remediation concerns the inclusion of nanomaterials within the polymeric systems (Ahmadijokani et al., 2022). Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), nanomaterials with a sp2 hybridized graphitic core and size typically less than 10 nm, have attracted increasing attention due to their excellent water solubility, strong photoluminescence, good conductivity, low-cost synthesis, biocompatibility, and chemical stability (Shandilya et al., 2021). They can be synthesized via top-down or bottom-up techniques. The first approach separates large graphite materials into smaller carbon-based particles. In contrast, the bottom-up approach requires molecular precursors, such as citric acid, that undergo carbonization and polymerization to form CQDs (Han et al., 2020). An eco-friendly solution synthesizes such particles from low-cost precursors (F et al., 2022), such as agricultural waste that pollutes the environment if not managed (Tohamy, 2024). To reduce pollution from such waste, researchers are increasingly trying to transform it into valuable products, as recently demonstrated by developing carbon nanomaterials using mature sawdust (SCB) or sugarcane bagasse as feedstock (Tohamy et al., 2023a). Obtaining CQDs from such waste involves using an economical, green, and single-step procedure with the aid of microwave heating of urea (Seedad et al., 2021). This approach offers a sustainable way to address environmental concerns while creating functional materials (Tohamy, 2023). CQDs can be doped using non-metallic elements, such as nitrogen, sulfur, and potassium. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N–CQDs) have a large surface area and a particular core. This core is surrounded by functional groups containing oxygen and nitrogen that make the CQDs water-soluble despite the core being water-repellent. As a result, functionalized CQDs are well-dispersed in water and biocompatible (Mowafi and Tohamy, 2023). Scientists have developed various methods to synthesize CQDs, such as heating organic molecules with hydrothermal and solvothermal treatments, using lasers to break down graphite, and pyrolytic carbonization. Microwave heating is gaining popularity as a more efficient and economical approach for synthesizing CQDs. CQDs are emerging additives for handling urbanized and industrial wastewater (Tohamy et al., 2023b). CQDs, being nanoscale, can reinforce separation membranes by dispersing more uniformly within the membrane matrix than large-sized fillers (Yuan et al., 2018). This uniform dispersion helps maintain membrane structure, preventing aggregates or voids. CQDs' surface chemistry and functional groups can be tailored to create transport sites that selectively adsorb or repel specific molecules (Long et al., 2021). Electrospun membranes based on cellulose acetate reinforced with N-CQDs obtained from sugarcane bagasse were produced to treat wastewater using green methods. N-CQDs were synthesized via microwave heating with eco-friendly urea as a source of nitrogen elements. N-CQDs were added to a Cellulose acetate polymer solution to produce the membrane for effective adsorption of various cationic dyes, such as methylene blue, methyl violet, and rhodamine B.

Sugarcane bagasse (SCB) waste was attained from Quena Company for the Paper Industry (Quena, Egypt). Cellulose acetate (CA), NaOH, urea, acetic acid, dimethylformamide (DMF), methylene blue (MB), methyl violet (MV), and Rhodamine B (RhB) were acquired from Merck KGaA (Darmstadt, Germany). NaCl and CaCl2 were purchased from Carlo Erba Reagents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Attainment of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: By mixing 30 mg of SCB with 70 mg of NaOH and 2400 mg of urea, N-CQDs were created from SCB. Afterward, the combination was frozen overnight to produce a dissolved solution, which was ultrasonicated for 2 min. The sonicated solution was heated at 700 W for about 7 min in a home microwave.

Electrospinning is a promising technique for producing nano and micro polymeric fibers for targeted applications using almost any type of polymer. Electrospinning is based on an electrohydrodynamic process, during which a liquid droplet is electrified to generate a charged jet, followed by stretching and elongation to produce fibers. Generally speaking, the electrospinning process can be divided into four steps: (i) charging of the liquid droplet followed by the formation of Taylor cone; (ii) extension of the charged jet in a straight segment; (iii) decrease in jet thickness due to the electric field and generation of electrical bending instability (whipping instability); and (iv) solidification and collection of fibers (Xue et al., 2019). To produce the electrospun membranes, a solution was prepared by dissolving cellulose acetate (12% w/w) in a mixture of acetic acid and DMF (4:1 v/v). The solution was thoroughly mixed at 50 °C (300 rpm) for 4 h until it became uniform. Once the polymer was solubilized entirely, N-CQDs (0.1, 0.5, and 1% w/w) were incorporated into the solution. The electro-spinning process was then conducted under optimized conditions to ensure the production of smooth and bead-free fibrous membranes. These conditions included a flow rate of 1 mL/h, a voltage of 21 kV, a needle-collector distance of 19 cm, a temperature of 25 °C, and a relative humidity of 35%. The fibrous membranes were produced using a climate-controlled electrospinning apparatus (EC-CLI, IME Technologies, Geldrop, The Netherlands) in a vertical setup. The diameter of the needle used for electrospinning was 0.8 mm.

The membranes obtained from the electrospinning process are named.
-
CA when the membrane consists of cellulose acetate only;
-
CA@CQDs-x where x = N-CQDs loading (%).
Fourier-transform infrared spectra were acquired using the KBr disk method, employing a Mattson 5000 spectrometer (Unicam, United Kingdom).
Using a JEOL JEM-2100 electron microscope (Akishima, Tokyo, Japan), transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were captured at an acceleration voltage of 120 kV.

The crystallinity was examined using X-ray powder diffraction, with the diffraction patterns collected by a Bruker D-8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Germany), operating at a 40 kV voltage and a current of 40 mA, employing copper (Kα) radiation with a wavelength of 1.5406 Å.

A thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted on the polymer powder using a PerkinElmer analyzer. The sample was heated to 800 °C at 10 °C per minute in a nitrogen atmosphere. The data from the thermal analysis were recorded to calculate the activation energy for thermal degradation. Equations (1) and (2) were utilized in accordance with the Coats-Redfern method:

To evaluate the data, the correlation between {log10 [1-(1-α)1−n]/T2 (1-n)} and 1/T was plotted for different n values, which should yield a straight-line relationship as described by equation (1). For n = 1, the connection between log {–log(1-α)]/T2} and 1/T was plotted according to equation (2). Subsequently, the least squares method was employed by choosing numerous n values (in the range 0–3.0), determining the correlation coefficient (R) for each n value, and assessing the standard error (SE). The frequency factor A was found from the intercept of the Coats-Redfern equation for the most appropriate n value, whereas the activation energy (E) was derived from the slope (Tohamy, 2023). Equation (3) were used to estimate other kinetic parameters, including enthalpy (ΔH), entropy (ΔS), and free energy change (ΔG).

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM and dimensional analysis of the membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the membranes was studied using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Prior to analysis, the samples were coated with a thin layer of gold through sputtering. The images were captured using a Phenom ProX microscope operating in high-vacuum mode. The imaging software Sigma Scan Pro 5 (Aspire Software International, Ashburn, VA, USA) was used to obtain the fiber distribution. About 500 diameters were collected and fitted using a log-normal distribution using the software Microcal Origin Pro 2021 (OriginLab Corporation, Northampton, MA, USA).

The Plot Profile plug-in of Fiji software was used to obtain plot profiles from SEM images, generating two-dimensional graphs that display pixel intensities within specified areas. The abscissa axis corresponds to distance, while the ordinate axis denotes pixel intensity. Finally, the surface roughness average (Ra), defined as the math mean of total profile height deviations from the mean line over the evaluation length, was computed using equation (4):
(4)

=

∫ 0 L
Z

where Z(x) is the profile height function.

Adsorption tests were performed using a batch process. Dye solutions were made by dissolving different cationic dyes in distilled water to achieve specific concentrations. Then, pre-weighed adsorbents were added to the dye solutions. The CQDs loading (0.1%–1%), the adsorbent dosage (1–10 g/L), the solution pH (3–11), the initial dye concentration in the solution (25–300 ppm), the temperature (25–80 °C), the salts' presence (NaCl and CaCl2) and the ionic strength were varied to test the effect of these parameters on the MB removal. The pH of the dye solutions was adjusted by adding NaOH (1 M) or HCl (1 M) solutions. The effect of ionic strength on adsorption capacity was evaluated, fixing the optimized experimental parameters, by testing the adsorption performances of CA@CQDs in different NaCl and CaCl2 solutions (1, 5, and 10 mg/L). Adsorption tests were performed by shaking the samples at a fixed temperature for 24 h. Subsequently, the dye concentrations in the solutions were evaluated by measuring the absorbance using the UV–Vis technique at 664 nm for MB, 590 nm for MV, and 554 nm for RhB.

The adsorption capacity (qt) and efficiency removal of dyes were assessed using the following equations (5) and (6):

where c0 is the initial dye concentration, ct is the concentration at time t, V is the volume of the dye solution, and m is the mass of the membrane.

pH value at the point of zero charge (pHPZC) was evaluated to understand the effect of pH on the adsorption performances of the electrospun CA membrane. The point of zero charge (PZC) is the pH of the solution at which the net surface charge is zero. An aliquot of 40 mL NaNO3 solution (0.1 M) was collected in ten flasks. The pH was set from 2 to 11 using HCl (1M) and NaOH (1M) drops. Then, 200 mg of the adsorbent was placed inside the flasks and shaken for 24 h. After that, the CA-based adsorbent was taken out, and the final pH (pHf) was measured by using a pH meter (Crison-pH-Burette 24 1S).

The change in pH was calculated as follows (equation (7)):

A plot of ΔpH versus pHi was obtained, and the pHPZC was easily estimated by the intercept on the x-axis of the curve.

Desorption studies were carried out by agitating the used CA@CQDs adsorbent with ethanol for 3 h at 400 rpm. The adsorbent was then dried at 90 °C. After that, the regenerated adsorbent was ready to be used further to remove MB in subsequent adsorption tests. After each cycle, the efficiency removal (%) of MB was evaluated.
Fig. 1 depicts the outcomes of the characterization of N-CQDs using TEM, FTIR spectroscopy, XRD analysis, and TGA/DTG.

As depicted in Fig. 1a, TEM investigation revealed that N-CQDs are balled–shaped with sizes between 2.35 and 2.90 nm. The detailed FTIR spectrum of the N-CQDs is shown in Fig. 1b: it revealed absorption bands at 3434 (N–H), 3332 (O–H), 1675 (amide I), 1589 (amide II), 1457 (C=C), 1299 (O–C=O), 1151 (C–O–C) and 1045.39 (C–N) cm−1. N-H and C-N vibrations’ presence proves N-CQDs preparation (Tohamy, 2023). Fig. 1c reports the XRD graph; from the curve, it is possible to observe that N-CQDs showed a broad peak at 18.83 related to the (002) plane (JCPDS card no. 26–1076) (F et al., 2022; Tohamy et al., 2023b).

Fig. 1d shows the TGA/DTG of N-CQDs, which revealed four thermal decomposition stages from 25 to 1000 °C. The initial stage between 40.66 and 172.04 °C can be ascribed to the evaporation of the moisture contained in the sample. The second and third stages occupied 239.27–354.73 °C and 353.88–410.03 °C, respectively. These stages were attributed to the release of organic materials, most likely associated with the carbon core surface (Tohamy, 2023; Rossi et al., 2022). Furthermore, the E value for the second stage (32.35 kJ mol−1) was higher, whereas the E value for the third stage (21.33 kJ mol−1) implies that the second phase is related to carbon core burn (Rossi et al., 2022). In the fourth stage, the carbonaceous core of N-CQDs was oxidatively degraded between 410.66 and 441.68 °C. Detailed data are reported in Table s1.

Fig. 2 a and b report the SEM micrographs of the obtained samples; both samples show high porosity with randomly arranged micrometer-sized fibers. The uniform distribution of CA fiber diameters (Fig. 2a) could be due to the strong interactions between the polymers; in the case of N-CQDs-loaded CA membranes (Fig. 2b), the fibers appeared disordered, probably due to the existence of polar groups and aromatic rings in the assembly of CQDs which contribute to increasing the conductivity of the solution, causing flow instability and consequent formation of irregular fibers. It is also possible to observe that the unloaded sample and the systems loaded with N-CQDs have no surface defects. This proves that no phase separation arose during membrane formation, contributing to the fibers' smooth and regular surface. Then, it can be supposed that CA and CQDs can interact with each other through H-bonding, as well as electrostatic interactions between N-based groups of the CQDs and the carboxyl groups on the CA surface can lead to the attachment of the CQDs to the surface of the polymer. The CA polymer chain interacts with the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of CQDs, where CQDs act as the bridge, resulting in the entanglement of CA chains (Yang et al., 2022). Subsequently, the various systems' fiber diameter distributions were attained (Fig. 2c). It is possible to observe how the presence of N-CQDs leads to a reduction in the mean fiber diameter of around 1 μm. In comparison, the average diameter for the unloaded CA membranes is approximately 1.2 μm. The incorporation of CQDs slightly reduced the fiber diameter, which can be ascribed to the increase in the electrical conductivity of the polymeric solution and the affecting of Taylor's cone (Hazarika et al., 2024). However, the N-CQDs loaded membranes showed an increase in the standard deviation of the curves; this is linked to the disorder generated by the presence of N-CQDs in solution, which are materials with high conductive capacity. Moreover, the surface roughness was analyzed (Fig. 2d), and it is possible to observe notable differences in profile height. The roughness average (Ra) and root mean square (RMS) values are 130.6 and 145.8 for CA and 136.3 and 157.2 for CA@CDQs. The smaller diameter and the disordered distribution of the fibers contribute to increasing the surface roughness of CA@CQDS compared to CA. This results in improved binding of MB on the surface of CQDs-loaded CA electrospun membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of contact time, CQDs loading, adsorbent dosage, and initial concentration
ce:section.ce:para: The time required to achieve equilibrium is a crucial parameter in the design of economical water remediation processes (Chen et al., 2010). In Fig. 3 a, the effect of contact time on MB adsorption by the CA and the CA@CQDs (with different CQDs loadings) membranes is shown. Over 1% of CQDs, it was not possible to achieve the stability of the electrospinning process due to the too-high conductivity of the solution which made the process unstable. Moreover, 1% was set as the maximum concentration based on good dispersion stability in the solvent system used for electrospinning, which was not retained at higher concentrations. Time optimization for the maximum removal of MB onto CA membranes and CA loaded with CQDs membranes was done by varying contact time (1–24 h). The initial concentration, adsorbent dosage, and temperature were 50 ppm, 1 g/L, and T = 298 K, respectively, at the natural pH of the dye solution. The adsorption of the MB dye adsorbent consists of two phases: rapid adsorption (0–30 min) ascribed to the interaction of dye ions with available adsorption active sites and the steep concentration gradient between the liquid and solid phases (Viscusi et al., 2022a) and the final stage with a relatively slow adsorption rate due to the saturation of available adsorption sites (Viscusi et al., 2022a). The adsorption capacity showed a rapid increase with contact time initially before approaching equilibrium. The maximum adsorption capacity was reached after a contact time of 6h. Beyond this value, no noticeable differences can be observed. This finding has practical implications for developing efficient and cost-effective water remediation processes. The presence of CQDs in the membranes significantly increases the adsorption capacity, changing from 6.3 to 24.2, 37.3, and 49.3 mg/g for 0, 0.1, 0.5, and 1% of CQDs loading CA electrospun membranes. It is clear that as the CQDs loading increases the adsorption capacity improves due to the higher availability of adsorption sites offered by CQDs. Since 1% appeared to be the most promising concentration, hereinafter, the adsorption tests will be carried out using the sample CA@CQDS-10. Therefore, the influence of the other parameters on the adsorption capacity of CA@CQDs-10 was evaluated. A significant factor in adsorption studies is the solid/liquid ratio (S/L), which determines the adsorbent capacity for a given initial concentration of pollutant in solution. Fig. 3b illustrates the impact of adsorbent dosage on the percentage removal of MB dye. The influence of the adsorbent dose on dye removal efficiency was examined by changing the adsorbent dose between 1 and 10 g/L (c0 = 50 ppm, T = 25 °C). The removal percentage enlarged significantly with higher adsorbent doses. The MB removal percentage rose from 59% to 79% as the adsorbent dose was increased from 1 g/L to 10 g/L. This increase in removal percentage can be attributed to the larger available surface area for sorption and the greater number of adsorption sites, which facilitate more MB attachment as the adsorbent amount increases (Chen et al., 2010). Despite the excessive amount of adsorbent, which can cause unsaturation of sorption sites, a suitable binding between MB ions and the adsorbent can be postulated. The impact of varying initial MB concentrations, ranging from 25 to 300 mg/L, on the adsorption process was also investigated. The results are shown in Fig. 3c, demonstrating that MB adsorption depends on the dye concentration. A higher initial dye concentration establishes a higher driving force, which may overcome the MB's mass transfer resistance between the liquid and solid phases (Li et al., 2020; Tafese et al., 2024). The obtained results could even be supported by stating that the increase in the initial concentration favors the collision between MB ions and the adsorbent. The adsorption capacity of MB increased from 24.8 to 49.3, 90.8, 136.7 and 198.6 mg/g as the c0 is 25, 50, 100, 200 and 300 ppm, respectively. Besides, the R % is supposed to decrease since a higher initial MB concentration is responsible for the fast saturation of available surface sites of a fixed weight of adsorbent (Banerjee et al., 2016).

The equilibrium experimental data for MB adsorption onto CA@CQDs-10 electrospun adsorbent were fitted using different isothermal models: Langmuir (equation (8)) (Farghali et al., 2013), Freundlich (equation (9)) (Liu et al., 2012), Temkin (equation (10)) (Njoku et al., 2014; Arris et al., 2012), Redlich-Peterson (equation (11)) (Arris et al., 2012), Harkins Jura (equation (12)) (Arris et al., 2012), and Dubinin-Radushkevich (equation (13)) (Arris et al., 2012; Saruchi, 2019).

Fig. 4 shows the adsorption isotherm data (T = 25 °C and r = 1 g/L).
Table 1 reports the parameters evaluated from the fitting.

Langmuir model fits the adsorption data with R2 = 0.951. The qmax and kL parameters are 240.38 mg/g and 0.017 L/mg, respectively. As KL values reflect the equilibrium constant for the adsorption process, they show the adsorbent's affinity for dye. The feasibility of the adsorption process can be conveyed in terms of
R L
(equation (15)) value, which can be defined as a dimensionless constant separation factor (WeberJr and Morris, 1963):
(15)
R L
=
1
1 +
K L
C 0

If
R L
is in the range of 0–1, the adsorption is considered favorable (Abd El-Latif and Elkady, 2010). Correspondingly, the
R L
values presented in Table 2 indicate the favorable removal of MB by electrospun adsorbent.

The Freundlich isotherm, which describes adsorption on heterogeneous surfaces, fits the data with good reliability (R2 = 0.992). This model can be used for non-ideal sorption involving heterogeneous adsorption (Santhi et al., 2016). The 1/n value, which is 0.68, proves the favorability of the adsorption process. A value for 1/n < 1 indicates concurrence with Langmuir isotherm. A non-uniform distribution of adsorption sites could generate changes in the energy level on the surface of the adsorbent, leading to the formation of heterolayers. The Temkin and the Redlich-Peterson models shaped the adsorption data significantly. Given the low coefficient of determination, the Harkins-Jura and Dubinin-Radushkevich models did not fit the data with good reliability.

The pH plays an essential role in the adsorption process (Wang et al., 2005), since it affects the speciation of the dye in solution and the surface charge distribution of the solid adsorbent (Salazar-Rabago et al., 2017). The MB uptake was found to be dependent on pH level (Fig. 5 a). To better understand the effect of pH, the pH at the point of zero charge of the adsorbent (pHPZC), which is known to be a crucial factor in determining the surface charge of an adsorbent, was evaluated according to the methodology reported before (Fig. 5b).

The adsorption capacity increases as pH rises from pH = 3 to pH = 9. Interestingly, no change in the adsorption capacity was observed at the value of pH ≥ 7 (near the PZC value). Meanwhile, in highly basic environments, the qt appeared to decrease. To better understand the no-linear trend of qt as pH changes, the pH at the point of zero charge should be considered. The pHPZC value for the CA@CQDs adsorbent is roughly 8.2. It follows that, as pH > pHPZC, there is an excess of negative charge on the electrospun membrane, while as pH < pHPZC, there is an excess of positive charges on the surface (Li et al., 2020). In basic media (for pH > pHPZC), the excess of negative charge and the presence of OH− led to an improvement in adsorption capacity since the MB in solution exists as a cationic ion (MB+). Besides, in a highly basic environment, the nitrogen atom in the central cycle of MB molecule is completely deprotonated, and the MB dye becomes negatively charged (MB−) (Bensedira et al., 2022).

So, the formation of electrostatic interactions between the cationic dye and the negative active sites of the electrospun membrane surface could justify the increase in qt as pH increases. As the presence of hydroxyl groups increases, surface adsorption groups are predominantly deprotonated, resulting in a higher number of adsorption sites available for binding the cationic MB (Amode et al., 2016). At pH = 3, MB is mainly present as an undissociated species. The electrostatic attraction between the MB+ and the surface of the CA@CQDs did not favor the adsorption of MB. Therefore, other mechanisms concur with the adsorption. Below the pHPZC, the excess of positive charges on the adsorbent surface is responsible for generating repulsive interactions with the positive MB ions. Moreover, the competition between MB positive ions and H+ for the present adsorption sites even occurred, limiting the adsorption of MB ions (Yadav et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature and evaluation of thermodynamic parameters
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 shows the qt values referred to MB adsorption evaluated at different temperatures (25, 50, and 80 °C) for CA@CQDs-10.

The qt parameter decreases as temperature increases, suggesting that the adsorption of MB is favored at low temperatures. The decrease in adsorption capacity at elevated temperatures can be attributed to three primary factors. Firstly, the solubility of the dye tends to increase at higher temperatures, leading to interactions between the solvent and adsorbent that hinder the adsorption process. Secondly, the kinetic energy of the materials involved in adsorption is heightened at high temperatures, resulting in weaker interactions between the adsorbent and adsorbate. Lastly, the elevated temperature enhances the mobility of dye molecules within the solution, thereby influencing their adsorption. Conversely, at the standard ambient temperature of 298 K, adsorption occurs more spontaneously, as evidenced by the results in Fig. 6.

Thermodynamic parameters, such as enthalpy change
Δ H
°
(kJ mol−1) and entropy change
Δ S

(J mol−1 K−1) for MB adsorption on N-CQDs loaded CA membranes were evaluated to understand the system's feasibility through equation (16) (Tan et al., 2009):

is equal to −65.6 J mol−1 K−1. The values of ΔH° are negative, revealing that adsorption is an exothermic process. As T increases, the dye molecule diffusion is enhanced throughout the external and internal boundary layer of the adsorbent due to the reduction in the apparent viscosity of the solution (Abas and Al Kiey, 2023). Furthermore, the low values of ΔH° indicate that the energies involved in dye adsorption are weak, demonstrating the physical nature of the process (El-Shamy, 2020). The negative value of ΔS° refers to the reorganization of surface adsorption, which results in less randomness and a more regular pattern for MB adsorption.

The fitting plots obtained by applying equations (16)–(19) allowed to evaluate the kinetic parameters reported in Table 3 .

Correlation coefficients R2 were relatively high for the applied models. The lowest reliability was observed for the pseudo-first-order model, followed by the Brouser-Weron-Sotolongo model, which indicates a process that follows the chemisorption interactions type. The mixed first-second order model appeared to describe the kinetic data with satisfying reliability. In contrast, the most promising model is the pseudo-second-order model (R2 > 0.99) since the calculated adsorption capacities (qe) were very close to the experimental values. These results support the statement that the rate-limiting step may involve chemisorption, which is attributed to the interactions between the cationic MB and the functional groups of N-CQDs. The intraparticle diffusion model (equation (21)) is applied to determine if pore or film diffusion is the dominant step in the adsorption (Alkan et al., 2007).
(21)

The

1 / 2
plot was reported for each initial concentration and shown in Fig. 7 , while the k and C values are inserted in Table 4 . The values of C provide information on the thickness of the boundary layer; it is also known that the resistance to the external mass transfer increases as the intercept rises (Santhi et al., 2016).

The intraparticle diffusion model concerns three phenomena (bulk diffusion, intraparticle diffusion, and equilibrium) represented by three different slopes (Li et al., 2016). Fig. 7 shows that three linear regions were observed. The process is characterized initially by the boundary layer diffusion (step 1) with a high kid since the adsorbent can fast adsorb a high amount of adsorbate molecules. Over time, due to the accumulation of adsorbate, the concentration gradients decrease, leading to a decrease in the adsorption rate. In all cases, the first stage, associated with the diffusion through the boundary layer, is characterized by high ki1, which increases as c0 changes from 25 ppm to 300 ppm.

Besides, the second step showed a lower kid, which indicated a slower adsorption rate due to the intraparticle diffusion. The ki2 follows the same trend as ki1, rising as c0 increases. The third stage concerns the final equilibrium stage. The rate constants ki1 were the highest. Therefore, the boundary layer's diffusion resistance was lower than that of pore diffusion. The constant C (for steps 2 and 3) increased with dye concentration, revealing an increase in boundary layer thickness. Since the plot does not pass through the origin, intraparticle diffusion is not the only process to be considered. Therefore, the process can be controlled by both external diffusion and intraparticle diffusion.

Boyd equation can be used to understand the rate-controlling step (equation (22)):

The plot of Boyd as Bt vs. t is reported in the supplementary materials Figure s1a. If the plot gives a straight line passing through the origin, the pore diffusion is the controlling mechanism. In contrast, film diffusion or external mass transport will be the main dominating factor (Hameed and El-Khaiary, 2008; Yao and Chen, 2017). Bangham and Burt's model was even applied. The linearity of this model is proof of the fact that the adsorption is pore-diffusion-controlled (Mehmood et al., 2015). This model is described through equation (24):

where Ci represents the initial dye concentration (mg/L), m denotes the mass of the adsorbent (g), V stands for the volume (L), qt signifies the adsorption capacity at time t (mg/g) and α2 and kb are constants. The fitting curves obtained by applying Bangham and Burt's model are reported in Figure s1b, while α2 and kb values are reported in Table 5 .

The values of R2 of Boyd's equation ranged from 0.85 to 0.99. None of the curves passes through the origin. Therefore, it can be assumed that the adsorption of MB onto electrospun CA@CQDs is not a pore diffusion-controlled mechanism. Regarding Bangham and Burt's model, the R2 values are lower than the one reported for Boyd's model. Correlation coefficients suggest that Bangham's equation is not strictly linear. Therefore, the hypothesis of pore diffusion being the sole rate-controlling process is dismissed, reinforcing the conclusion that film diffusion plays a crucial role in controlling the process rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of salinity and ionic strength
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 shows the effects of two different electrolytic solutions (NaCl and CaCl2) and the ionic strength on the adsorption of MB by using CA@CQDs (the initial concentration, adsorbent dosage, and temperature were 50 ppm, 1 g/L, and T = 298 K, respectively, at the natural pH of the dye solution).

The ionic strength generated by adding different salts into the MB solution could drastically interfere with the electrostatic interactions by the screening effect (Ahmadijokani et al., 2023). Fig. 8 highlights that the adsorption amount, at a set concentration, is higher for NaCl solution compared to CaCl2 one, indicating that the cations can alter the surface property and thus can affect the adsorption of MB. The hydrated radius of sodium is smaller than calcium one, tending to hinder the dye binding less (Cheng et al., 2018). Moreover, increasing the salt concentration resulted in a decrease in MB adsorption, ascribed to the screening effect and the competition between the MB cations and Na + or Ca2+ ions to occupy the surface sites. The negative impact of ionic strength on dye uptake might suggest the possibility of ion exchange mechanisms determined by cations competition in the sorption process (Maurya et al., 2006). Then, the activity of the methylene blue is known to be reduced as ionic strength increases (Han et al., 2006), supporting the reported data.

Desorption and regeneration studies are quite crucial for evaluating the economic feasibility of the process. The recovery (%) of MB over the number of adsorption/desorption cycles was reported in Fig. 9 .

Results showed a 2 % drop in the adsorption capacity after the first regeneration cycle. The R% slightly decreased for the consecutive cycles (up to 5 cycles) showing that the adsorbent could be used again and again without any further loss in its performance. The regenerated CA@CQDs adsorbent still showed relatively high removal efficiencies of 91% toward cationic MB dye after 5 cycles, demonstrating the applicability of adsorbents in the removal of pollutants from wastewater.

Organic dyes are widely used, and dye-polluted water poses a serious threat to the environment and human health. Two other dyes, methyl violet and rhodamine B, were used to evaluate the dye-capture ability of CA@CQDs. The adsorption experiments were performed for 24 h, and the absorbance curves were measured (Fig. 10 ).

As shown in Fig. 10c and d, the dark violet solution of MV faded to light violet after 24 h. Besides, for RhB, a decrease in color intensity from reddish to pink was observed. These outcomes are consistent with the UV/vis spectroscopic results showing a reduction in the intensity of the main peak at the characteristic wavelength (Fig. 10a and b). The analysis of absorbance curves allowed the evaluation of the recovery % for both systems, up to 88% for MV and 69% for RhB. The results prove the produced system's potential adsorption ability towards removing cationic dyes.
The reaction mechanism is based on the following equation:

where S is the adsorbent, D is the dye, and SD∗ is the intermediate unstable complex. The adsorption process should be studied by considering the overlap of several factors, such as the dye structure and surface area. A possible mechanism was proposed based on the reported data (Fig. 11 ).

The N-CQDs’ adsorptive capacity is considerably affected by their surface structure, with crucial roles played by the mass transfer process and the physical and chemical characteristics of these materials. Adsorption of MB is likely due to weak electrostatic bonds between positive MB molecules and negative groups on the surfaces of CA@CQDs. These functional groups act as binding sites for MB adsorption. Specifically, electrostatic forces between positively charged MB and negatively charged N-CQDs and π–π stacking interactions between the C=C bonds of MB and π electrons of aromatic groups contribute to the adsorption process. Additionally, hydrogen bonds involving -OH and/or -COOH functional groups on N-CDQs surfaces and nitrogen atoms of methylene blue, as well as Yoshida H-bonding, further enhance MB binding to CQDs. It follows that the presence of CQDs increases the density of oxygen-containing functional groups, facilitating electrostatic interactions and π–π electron coupling.
Fig. 11b displays the FTIR spectra of the adsorbent before and after MB adsorption.

The spectrum of CA@CQDs shows characteristic bands, including C-H stretching (2950–2890 cm−1), C=O stretching of the carbonyl group (1756 cm−1), C-CH3 stretching (1372 cm−1), and C-O-C anti-symmetric stretching of the ester group (1260 cm−1). Peaks at 1457 cm−1 (C=C), 1280 cm−1 (O–C=O), 1177 cm−1 (C–O–C), and 1050 cm−1 (C–N) indicate the presence of CQDs. The decrease in intensity of the C=O carbonyl stretching band at 1756 cm−1 or the C-O band at 1085 cm−1 provides evidence of these groups’ involvement in MB adsorption. After adsorption, peaks at 2924 cm−1 (C-H) and 1597 cm−1 (CH=N) characteristic of MB are visible in the spectrum (Alshehri and Malik, 2019). The SEM micrograph (Fig. 11c) confirms effective MB adsorption on electrospun fibers, showing the presence of some agglomerates.
To propose a comparison with other electrospun adsorbents, the maximum adsorption capacities (qm, mg/g) of similar systems are listed in Table 6 .

By analyzing the data reported in the table, the adsorption performances of the electrospun nitrogen-doped CQDs loaded CA adsorbent are higher than or comparable to those of the reported systems, proving that the designed adsorbent has a great capability for the adsorption of cationic dyes from aqueous solutions.

The electrospinning process is successfully used to produce the nitrogen-doped CQDs, obtained from agro-waste resources, loaded cellulose acetate membrane as a novel efficient adsorbent. The novelty of this work is placed on the design of a multifunctional adsorbent for removing several cationic dyes such as methylene blue, rhodamine B, and methyl violet. To assess the performance of the membranes, a series of batch adsorption tests were conducted using MB as a model pollutant, examining variables such as CQDs loading (from 0.1 to 1% wt), contact time (ranging from 1 to 24 h), initial concentration of methylene blue (ranging from 25 to 300 mg/mL), solution pH (3–11) and adsorbent doses (ranging from 1 to 10 g/L).

The MB adsorption capacity increases with the increase in CQDs loading due to the higher availability of adsorption sites. The results demonstrated that MB adsorption depends on the dye concentration, while the maximum adsorption capacity of the membrane, evaluated through the Langmuir model, was 198 mg/g. The MB uptake was found to be dependent on pH level, rising as pH increases up to pH = 9 mainly due to the generation of electrostatic interactions, even if other mechanisms could occur. Besides, the MB adsorption was exothermic, showing a reduction as T increases. The pseudo-second-order model appeared to describe the kinetic data with high reliability (R2 > 0.99), and the calculated adsorption capacity was very close to the experimental ones. Besides, the presence of salt ions affected the adsorption capacity, leading to its decrease. The CA@CQDs membranes efficiently removed other cation dyes such as methyl violet (removal = 88%) and rhodamine B (removal = 69%). A potential adsorption mechanism was even reported. This investigation revealed that the N-doped carbon quantum dots really improve the removal efficiency of pollutants from wastewater using effective and ecological methods with great reusability (the removal efficiency drops by about 8 % after 5 cycles). The novelty of this study relies on obtaining new composite material based on N-doped CQDs from agro waste resources and cellulose acetate electrospun membranes as efficient adsorbent of cationic organic dyes.
